title: FTC Operation AI Comply
proceduralStage: announced enforcement actions
affectedActorClass: AI products, AI claims, and AI-enabled deceptive schemes
outcomeOrRemedy: Five law-enforcement actions announced; outcomes vary by matter.
summary: FTC enforcement sweep against deceptive AI claims and schemes.
caveat: Enforcement context only; no tracked frontier lab is treated as a target without an official source.
verificationNotes: Official FTC release announces five law-enforcement actions and states that using AI tools does not exempt firms from existing law.

title: FTC Rytr AI review-generator proposed order
proceduralStage: FTC administrative complaint and proposed order
affectedActorClass: AI writing assistant provider and review/testimonial generation service
outcomeOrRemedy: Proposed order would bar offering a service dedicated or promoted for generating consumer reviews or testimonials.
summary: FTC Operation AI Comply matter alleging that Rytr's AI writing assistant enabled false and deceptive consumer reviews.
caveat: Consumer-protection enforcement context; it is not a frontier-lab matter and does not create a new AI-specific statute.
verificationNotes: Official FTC release identifies Rytr as an AI writing assistant matter, describes alleged fake-review generation, and summarizes the proposed order.

title: FTC Rite Aid facial-recognition order
status: stipulated order filed; case listed as pending on FTC page
proceduralStage: federal court case and FTC stipulated order
affectedActorClass: Retail deployer using facial recognition for security and surveillance
outcomeOrRemedy: Five-year prohibition on facial-recognition use for security or surveillance plus automated-system safeguards and information-security obligations.
summary: FTC case involving Rite Aid's use of facial recognition technology and safeguards for automated biometric systems.
caveat: Existing-law enforcement context for biometric AI; no frontier lab is treated as a party or target.
sourceName: FTC - Rite Aid Corporation, FTC v.
verificationNotes: Official FTC case page lists the AI/facial-recognition tags, docket details, and case summary describing the five-year facial-recognition prohibition and safeguards.

title: SEC AI-washing settlements with investment advisers
proceduralStage: SEC settled enforcement actions
outcomeOrRemedy: $400,000 in total civil penalties across two settling investment advisers.
summary: SEC settled charges against investment advisers for allegedly false and misleading AI-use statements.
caveat: Securities-enforcement context for AI claims; it does not establish a general AI-specific statute.
verificationNotes: Official SEC release announces settled charges against Delphia and Global Predictions for false and misleading AI-use statements and total civil penalties.

title: FTC DoNotPay AI-lawyer deceptive-claims order
proceduralStage: FTC final order
affectedActorClass: Consumer AI service making professional-substitution claims
outcomeOrRemedy: Order prohibiting deceptive professional-substitution claims, imposing monetary relief, and requiring notice to affected subscribers.
summary: FTC case against DoNotPay over claims that its AI service could substitute for a human lawyer.
caveat: Consumer-protection enforcement context; not a general finding about all legal-AI products or frontier labs.
verificationNotes: Official FTC case page describes the AI-lawyer claims, settlement, final order, monetary relief, and notice requirement.

title: Dutch DPA Clearview AI facial-recognition fine
proceduralStage: Dutch data-protection authority decision
outcomeOrRemedy: Fine decision concerning Clearview AI facial-recognition data collection; exact continuing procedural posture should be checked manually.
summary: Dutch Data Protection Authority decision concerning Clearview AI's facial-recognition data collection.
caveat: Official page can reject automated retrieval; keep as medium confidence until manually spot-checked in the next source review.
sourceName: Dutch Data Protection Authority - Clearview AI fine decision
verificationNotes: Official Dutch DPA URL was identified by the source report but automated access returned a rejection; row remains medium confidence and should not be used as a frontier-lab legal exposure claim.

title: Korean PIPC DeepSeek status examination
status: recommendations for correction and improvement announced
proceduralStage: PIPC status examination results
affectedActorClass: Foreign generative-AI service provider operating in Korea
outcomeOrRemedy: Recommendations on cross-border transfer legal bases, deletion of certain transferred user-entered data, Korean privacy policy disclosure, child-data checks, safeguards, and domestic-agent designation.
summary: Korean PIPC status-examination results and recommendations concerning DeepSeek's R1 LLM chatbot service in Korea.
caveat: Privacy regulator examination and recommendations; legal effect should be read from the official PIPC release and underlying PIPA, not generalized to all frontier labs.
verificationNotes: Official PIPC release states the commission concluded deliberations on DeepSeek status-examination results and issued recommendations for correction and improvement.

title: FTC In the Matter of Everalbum, Inc.
proceduralStage: FTC administrative consent order
outcomeOrRemedy: Consent order over deceptive facial-recognition use and retention of photos/videos after account deactivation.
summary: FTC alleged Everalbum deceived users about facial-recognition use and retained content after deactivation; resolved by consent order (Matter No. 192 3172).
caveat: Consumer-protection / biometric enforcement context; no frontier lab is a party.
sourceName: FTC - Everalbum, Inc., In the Matter of
verificationNotes: FTC case page confirms matter number, complaint/decision dates, and subject matter.

title: EEOC v. iTutorGroup, Inc.
proceduralStage: Agency lawsuit and consent decree
outcomeOrRemedy: $365,000 settlement plus injunctive and compliance relief over software that auto-rejected older applicants (ADEA).
summary: EEOC's first AI-hiring-discrimination suit: iTutorGroup's tutor-application software allegedly auto-rejected applicants by age; settled for $365,000.
caveat: Employment-discrimination enforcement; outcome is a settlement, not an adjudicated liability finding.
verificationNotes: EEOC press releases confirm docket number, suit date, auto-rejection theory, and settlement amount.

title: FTC 6(b) inquiry into generative-AI investments and partnerships
proceduralStage: FTC Section 6(b) study
affectedActorClass: Major cloud providers and frontier-AI developers
outcomeOrRemedy: Compulsory 6(b) orders issued to five companies to study partnership terms and market impact.
summary: FTC issued compulsory 6(b) orders to Alphabet, Amazon, Anthropic, Microsoft, and OpenAI to study generative-AI investments/partnerships. A study, not an allegation of wrongdoing.
caveat: Formal regulatory study, not an enforcement complaint or finding of any law violation.
sourceName: FTC - Inquiry into Generative AI Investments and Partnerships
verificationNotes: Official FTC release confirms the 6(b) orders and the five recipients.

title: Thomson Reuters v. ROSS Intelligence Inc.
status: partial summary judgment for plaintiffs on infringement and fair use (pre-trial)
proceduralStage: Memorandum opinion revising summary-judgment rulings
outcomeOrRemedy: Court granted Thomson Reuters most of its partial summary judgment on direct infringement and rejected ROSS's fair-use defense on key issues.
summary: Landmark AI-training-data copyright case: ROSS trained a legal-research tool on data derived from Westlaw headnotes. In Feb 2025 the court largely sided with Thomson Reuters on infringement and fair use.
caveat: Litigation status changes; this is a pre-trial summary-judgment posture as of 2025-02-11, not a final judgment.
sourceName: GovInfo - Thomson Reuters v. ROSS memorandum opinion
verificationNotes: Official court opinion confirms the AI-training theory, case number, and Feb 2025 summary-judgment posture.

title: Mobley v. Workday, Inc.
status: pending; major disparate-impact claims survived motion to dismiss
proceduralStage: Partial denial of motion to dismiss (Second Amended Complaint)
outcomeOrRemedy: Court let key ADEA applicant disparate-impact claims proceed against Workday's AI screening tools, dismissing some FEHA/ADA theories with leave to amend.
summary: Closely-watched case on whether an AI hiring-tools vendor can face disparate-impact liability; substantial age-discrimination claims survived dismissal.
caveat: Litigation status changes; this reflects a motion-to-dismiss posture, not a merits ruling.
verificationNotes: GovInfo order confirms case number, parties, algorithmic-screening allegations, and procedural posture.

title: The New York Times Co. v. Microsoft Corp. & OpenAI
proceduralStage: Opinion on motions to dismiss (coordinated news-publisher actions)
affectedActorClass: Frontier model provider and deployer (training + outputs)
outcomeOrRemedy: As of the last officially verified opinion (2025-04-04), the case remained pending after the partial motion-to-dismiss ruling: some claims survived, some were dismissed with prejudice, and some DMCA claims were dismissed without prejudice.
summary: The flagship news-copyright case over training and outputs of OpenAI GPT models (and Microsoft products using them). In April 2025 the court allowed core claims to proceed.
caveat: Reflects the last officially verified posture (2025-04-04 SDNY opinion), not a live-docket refresh; later docket events were not verified.
sourceName: SDNY - NYT v. Microsoft/OpenAI MTD opinion
verificationNotes: Official SDNY opinion normalizes the Times-action defendants (Microsoft Corporation; OpenAI, Inc.; OpenAI LP; OpenAI GP, LLC; OpenAI, LLC; OpenAI OpCo LLC; OpenAI Global LLC; OAI Corporation, LLC; OpenAI Holdings, LLC) and confirms the post-MTD posture. No later official docket development verified beyond this opinion.

title: Andersen v. Stability AI Ltd.
proceduralStage: Interlocutory order on anti-SLAPP motion; ongoing pretrial litigation
affectedActorClass: AI image-generation developer (training data / artists' works)
outcomeOrRemedy: Court denied DeviantArt's renewed anti-SLAPP motion; broader copyright/publicity case continues.
summary: Artists' class action alleging Stable Diffusion and related AI image tools were trained on their works and infringe copyright and right of publicity.
caveat: Held for human review: the cited GovInfo PDF was not confirmed to be the specific February 2024 order described. Do not rely on the procedural detail until a PACER/RECAP docket-authenticated order is obtained.
sourceName: GovInfo - Andersen v. Stability AI order
verificationNotes: Re-verification (2026-06-16) could not confirm the specific February 2024 order from the named docket source chain. sourceUrl/proceduralStage left unchanged pending a PACER/RECAP docket-authenticated document; this record stays in the human-review lane.

title: Getty Images v. Stability AI Ltd
proceduralStage: Interlocutory High Court judgment in ongoing IP litigation
outcomeOrRemedy: High Court judgment addressed interlocutory issues after Stability's earlier unsuccessful strike-out/summary-judgment application.
summary: UK copyright/database-right/trademark claim that Stability scraped Getty images to train Stable Diffusion and that outputs can infringe and bear Getty marks.
caveat: Litigation status changes; interlocutory posture as of 2025-01-14, not a final judgment.
sourceName: Courts and Tribunals Judiciary - Getty v Stability AI
verificationNotes: Official judiciary page confirms neutral citation, case number, parties, and claim nature.

title: ICO investigation into Snap 'My AI'
proceduralStage: Preliminary Enforcement Notice; final decision not to enforce
affectedActorClass: Platform deploying a generative-AI chatbot (built on OpenAI GPT)
outcomeOrRemedy: After a Preliminary Enforcement Notice and a revised DPIA, ICO concluded in June 2024 that no enforcement notice was warranted.
summary: ICO probed whether Snap adequately assessed data-protection risks (including to children) before launching the 'My AI' chatbot; concluded without an enforcement notice after Snap revised its DPIA.
caveat: Privacy-regulator investigation that concluded without enforcement; not a finding of unlawfulness.
verificationNotes: Official ICO decision confirms the 6 Oct 2023 PEN, the revised DPIA, and the 19 June 2024 decision not to issue an enforcement notice.

title: ICO enforcement against Clearview AI Inc.
status: on remand after Upper Tribunal restored ICO jurisdiction; substantive FtT appeal not verified as resolved
proceduralStage: Monetary penalty + enforcement notice (2022); remitted to First-tier Tribunal after Upper Tribunal appeal
outcomeOrRemedy: The 2022 enforcement notice and £7,552,800 monetary penalty were not finally quashed. In [2025] UKUT 319 (AAC) the Upper Tribunal allowed the ICO's appeal, set aside the First-tier Tribunal's 17 October 2023 jurisdiction ruling, and remitted the substantive appeal on the basis that the ICO had jurisdiction to issue the notices. No later merits disposition verified.
summary: ICO fined Clearview AI £7.55m and ordered it to delete UK data over scraping facial images into a searchable database. After tribunal proceedings, the Upper Tribunal restored the ICO's jurisdiction and remitted the appeal to the First-tier Tribunal.
caveat: Do not treat the 2022 penalty as either finally enforceable or finally quashed: the verified status is remittal after the Upper Tribunal restored ICO jurisdiction.
sourceName: ICO / Upper Tribunal - Clearview AI appeal judgment [2025] UKUT 319 (AAC)
verificationNotes: Upper Tribunal judgment (re-verified 2026-06-16) confirms jurisdiction was restored and the appeal remitted to the FtT. No later FtT merits judgment or proof of collection verified. Historical ICO FOIA response (ic-237191-g0t9.pdf) confirms payment of the penalty was stayed pending appeal as of 2023-06-27.

title: Garante provisional limitation on ChatGPT (OpenAI)
jurisdiction: Italy (Garante per la protezione dei dati personali)
status: provisional limitation imposed, then suspended; inquiry continued
proceduralStage: Emergency regulatory action followed by ongoing inquiry
outcomeOrRemedy: Immediate provisional limitation of ChatGPT processing of Italian users' data; later suspended subject to measures while the inquiry continued.
summary: First major EU regulator action against ChatGPT: the Italian Garante ordered a provisional processing limit in March 2023 over privacy and age-verification concerns, later lifted after OpenAI measures.
caveat: Investigation-stage event; paired with the later 2024 sanction record. Context only, not AI law.
verificationNotes: Official Garante statements confirm the 30 March 2023 provisional limitation and that the inquiry continued after it was suspended.

title: Garante sanction against OpenAI (ChatGPT), Decision No. 755/2024
status: decision set aside at first instance after Court of Rome judgment (2026-03-18); appeal not verified
proceduralStage: Agency sanction; opposition upheld at first instance; decision removed from publication
outcomeOrRemedy: Garante announced a €15,000,000 sanction and a six-month information campaign in December 2024. The official decision page now states that Decision No. 755 of 2 November 2024 was temporarily removed after Court of Rome judgment No. 4153/2026 (published 2026-03-18) upheld the opposition against the Garante decision. No appeal could be verified from accessible primary sources.
summary: The Italian Garante fined OpenAI €15m over ChatGPT data processing (Decision 755/2024); a 2026 Rome court judgment upheld OpenAI's opposition and the decision was removed from publication.
caveat: Do not state 'pending appeal' as fact: primary sources confirm only the first-instance reversal / removal from publication, not the appeal posture.
sourceName: Garante - decision page (Decision 755 removed after Rome judgment)
verificationNotes: Re-verified 2026-06-16: primary sources confirm the 2024 sanction and that the decision remains removed from publication after Rome judgment 4153/2026 (2026-03-18). No appeal stage could be verified from accessible official sources. Press release (docweb 10085432) supports the fine amount and campaign.

title: Joint investigation of OpenAI (PIPEDA Findings #2026-002)
proceduralStage: Complaint-triggered joint regulator investigation with formal findings
outcomeOrRemedy: Detailed findings on collection/use, consent, transparency, accuracy, access, and retention; some aspects well-founded, some conditionally resolved after mitigation.
summary: Canada's federal and three provincial privacy regulators jointly investigated ChatGPT and issued findings in 2026 on consent, transparency, accuracy, and data-handling.
caveat: Launch month (April 2023) is officially confirmed but not the exact day; the 2026-05-06 findings date is the anchor.
verificationNotes: Re-verified 2026-06-16: official findings state the OPC launched the complaint-based investigation in April 2023 and the joint investigations were initiated in May 2023, with no exact day given. The prior 2023-04-01 placeholder is not an exact date.

title: Joint investigation of Clearview AI (PIPEDA Findings #2021-001)
proceduralStage: Joint privacy investigation with formal findings
outcomeOrRemedy: Authorities found the matter well-founded and recommended Clearview cease offering the tool in Canada and delete Canadians' images and biometric arrays.
summary: Canadian privacy regulators found Clearview AI's scraping of facial images unlawful and recommended it stop and delete Canadian data.
caveat: Findings with recommendations; Clearview disputed the conclusions. Context only.
verificationNotes: Official findings confirm the recommendations and Clearview's disagreement; exact publication day approximated to Feb 2021.

title: OAIC determination on Clearview AI Inc.
jurisdiction: Australia (Office of the Australian Information Commissioner)
proceduralStage: Privacy determination following joint investigation with UK ICO
outcomeOrRemedy: OAIC found Clearview breached Australians' privacy by scraping biometric information.
summary: OAIC determined (Privacy 2021 AICmr 54) that Clearview AI breached Australian privacy law by scraping facial images into a facial-recognition tool.
caveat: Privacy determination context; not a court judgment.
verificationNotes: OAIC materials confirm the joint investigation and the 14 October 2021 determination.

title: OAIC determination on Bunnings Group Limited
jurisdiction: Australia (Office of the Australian Information Commissioner)
proceduralStage: Privacy determination
affectedActorClass: Retail deployer using facial recognition via CCTV
outcomeOrRemedy: OAIC found privacy breaches and ordered Bunnings not to repeat or continue collecting biometric data without consent.
summary: OAIC found Bunnings collected customers' biometric (facial) data without consent across 63 stores and ordered it to stop.
caveat: Privacy determination context; Bunnings had a right to seek review.
verificationNotes: OAIC media release confirms scope (63 stores) and the review right.

title: OAIC determination on Kmart Australia Limited
jurisdiction: Australia (Office of the Australian Information Commissioner)
proceduralStage: Privacy determination after 2022 investigation
affectedActorClass: Retail deployer using facial recognition (refund-fraud control)
outcomeOrRemedy: OAIC found Kmart breached privacy law and rejected reliance on the fraud-prevention exemption on the pleaded facts.
summary: OAIC found Kmart's use of facial recognition to fight refund fraud (28 stores, 2020-2022) unlawfully collected biometric data.
caveat: Privacy determination context; not a court judgment.
verificationNotes: OAIC release confirms the system operated in 28 stores 2020-2022 and the investigation start in 2022.

title: ANPD preventive measure against Meta (generative-AI training)
jurisdiction: Brazil (Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados)
status: processing resumed subject to restrictions and ongoing ANPD monitoring
proceduralStage: Preventive measure, reconsideration, then compliance-based modification
outcomeOrRemedy: ANPD suspended the prohibition after approving Meta's compliance plan. Currently-operative restrictions: no use of personal data from accounts of users under 18 for generative-AI training; clear email and in-app notices; updated privacy disclosures; simplified objection rights for users and non-users; at least 30 days between notifications and the start of processing of public account data; and ongoing ANPD monitoring.
summary: Brazil's ANPD ordered Meta to stop using Brazilians' data to train generative AI over fundamental-rights risk, then permitted a restricted resumption after approving a compliance plan (no under-18 data, 30-day notice, opt-out, ongoing monitoring).
caveat: Latest officially verified ANPD position; restrictions could be amended by a later decision.
sourceName: ANPD - Meta may resume AI training with restrictions
verificationNotes: Re-verified 2026-06-16 against the ANPD news page and the Director's vote (Voto nº 23/2024/DIR-JR/CD): they specify the currently-operative restrictions. No later official amendment identified.

title: Japan PPC warning to OpenAI on ChatGPT
proceduralStage: Administrative warning under APPI Article 147 (not a monetary sanction)
outcomeOrRemedy: PPC issued a formal warning over acquisition of sensitive data without consent and notice/publication of purposes of use.
summary: Japan's PPC formally warned OpenAI in June 2023 about ChatGPT's handling of sensitive personal information and transparency of processing purposes.
caveat: Administrative warning, not an adjudicated sanction. Context only.
verificationNotes: Official PPC PDF states a 1 June 2023 warning to OpenAI entities under APPI Article 147.

title: ANI Media Pvt. Ltd. v. OpenAI OpCo LLC
status: judgment reserved on interim application; merits pending
proceduralStage: Court reserved judgment on interim application
summary: India's flagship news-publisher AI copyright case: ANI's claim against OpenAI over use of its content; the Delhi High Court reserved judgment on the interim application in March 2026.
caveat: No post-27-March-2026 dispositive order was verified; the Delhi High Court case-history portal is captcha-gated and could not be exhaustively checked.
sourceName: High Court of Delhi - ANI v OpenAI order
verificationNotes: Re-verified 2026-06-16: the official 2026-03-27 order confirms judgment was reserved on the interim application (I.A. 45300/2024). Post-order docket activity could not be exhaustively verified because the official Delhi High Court case-history interfaces require captcha.
